title: Self-Esteem Thought Record
description: Use this worksheet to identify, examine, and reframe self-critical thoughts. By separating facts from feelings and looking at the evidence, you can develop a more balanced and compassionate relationship with yourself.
instructions: When you notice a self-critical thought or a dip in self-worth, pause and work through each step below. Start by describing the situation, then capture the exact self-critical thought. Rate your emotions, identify the cognitive distortion at work, examine the evidence on both sides, and create a kinder, more balanced alternative.

fields.label: 1. Situation — What was happening?
fields.placeholder: Describe the event, interaction, or moment that triggered the self-critical thought...

fields.label: 2. Self-Critical Thought — What did your inner critic say?
fields.placeholder: Write the exact self-critical thought, e.g., 'I'm not good enough', 'I'll never get this right'

fields.label: 4. Emotion Intensity — How strong was the feeling? (0 = none, 100 = most intense)
5. Cognitive Distortion — Which thinking pattern is at work?
6. Evidence For — What supports this self-critical thought?
fields.placeholder: List specific facts (not feelings) that seem to support the thought...

fields.label: 7. Evidence Against — What contradicts this thought?
fields.placeholder: List facts, past successes, things others have said, or alternative explanations...

fields.label: 8. Balanced Thought — What would you say to a friend in this situation?
fields.placeholder: Write a kinder, more balanced alternative that accounts for the full picture...

fields.label: 9. Re-rate Emotion — How intense is the feeling now? (0 = none, 100 = most intense)

distortionsList.name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
distortionsList.description: Seeing yourself in black-and-white terms: 'I'm a total failure' or 'I'm perfect.' No room for the messy, human middle ground.

distortionsList.name: Mental Filter
distortionsList.description: Only noticing the negatives — a single criticism drowns out a dozen compliments. The positive evidence gets filtered out entirely.

distortionsList.name: Disqualifying the Positive
distortionsList.description: Dismissing achievements, compliments, or positive experiences as luck, timing, or 'not counting' — keeping the negative self-view intact.

distortionsList.name: Emotional Reasoning
distortionsList.description: Treating feelings as facts: 'I feel worthless, so I must be worthless.' Emotions are real, but they are not always accurate reflections of reality.

distortionsList.name: Labeling
distortionsList.description: Defining your entire self by a single event or behavior: 'I'm stupid,' 'I'm a loser,' 'I'm broken' — rather than describing what happened.

distortionsList.name: Should Statements
distortionsList.description: Rigid rules about how you 'should' be: 'I should be further along,' 'I shouldn't need help,' 'I should be able to handle this.' These create guilt and shame.

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for professional mental health treatment. If self-critical thoughts are persistent and distressing, consider working with a therapist trained in CBT or compassion-focused therapy. If you are experiencing thoughts of self-harm or suicide, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988.
